source: plan/bearings.md § Article hero art — colorful keyboard SVGs
template: directive: simple line drawing + single splash of color illustrative of subject
title: Keyboard firmware, compared: QMK, VIA, and VIAL
subject: Top-down 75% keyboard silhouette with three ochre callout chips floating above (QMK, VIA, VIAL), each chip dashed-leading down to a distinct cluster of keys that the firmware governs — making the 'one keyboard, three firmware faces' claim visually rather than verbally.
background: transparent / warm-paper (renders cleanly in light and dark mode)
motif: Top-down 75% layout in warm-grey 2px family stroke (outer case, inset bezel, five-row key grid with arrow cluster + right nav column). Three ochre rounded-rect 'layer regions' overlaid on three key clusters (left third = QMK / compile-and-flash root layer; middle third = VIA / live-remap working layer; right third including arrow cluster and nav column = VIAL / open variant, device-stored layer). Three rounded-rect chip labels above the keyboard, each containing the firmware name rendered as path-built monospace-feel letterforms (no font dependency) — QMK with circular Q + cruciform tail, VIA with vee + serifed I + crossbar A, VIAL adding L. Faint dashed lead lines connect each chip down to its cluster, with small splash-hue landing ticks at the cluster top. Under each chip, three short splash-hue tick-bars stand in as a hand-drawn 'and here's what this one does' descriptor without committing to body type. Focal cursor (filled core r=6 + outer ring r=11) at the top centre of the middle (VIA) cluster as the family-convention anchor. Eight-tick baseline + four left-edge layer ticks per family rhythm. Brass-bronze theme dot at (1148, 92).
alt: Top-down outline of a 75% mechanical keyboard with three ochre labelled chips floating above it (QMK, VIA, VIAL), each connected by a dashed lead line to a different cluster of keys, suggesting that the three firmwares each control a different layer of the same physical keyboard.
prompt: Guides-pillar piece on choosing between QMK, VIA, and VIAL — the three dominant mechanical-keyboard firmware stacks. The article's load-bearing claim is that the three are not direct alternatives but a layered stack: QMK is the underlying compile-and-flash firmware, VIA is the live-remap GUI riding on top, VIAL is the open variant that moves the keymap onto the device. Visual metaphor: 'one keyboard, three firmware faces'. Following bearings hero-art directive, guides pieces lean concrete and process-shaped, so the subject is the actual thing (a top-down 75% layout — thock's silhouette template, already established by 75-percent-default) with three labelled callouts. Bearings recommended Option A (keyboard silhouette + three firmware labels with dashed connectors to key clusters); rendered exactly that. Splash hue is ochre oklch(0.72 0.13 75), warm and editorial without going tech-blue — sits 5 hue-degrees off the existing ochre 75 family (trends-tracker, beginners-switch-buying-guide) but at chroma 0.13 (between the 0.10 lubing amber and 0.135 bronze brass dot) so the family palette stays coherent. Composition mirrors 75-percent-default (the obvious neighbour): same warm-grey 2px outer case + inset bezel + five-row dot grid + arrow cluster + right nav column, but lowered into the bottom-mid canvas to leave the upper third for the three callout chips. Chip letterforms are drawn as paths (lines + a single circle for Q) rather than text — keeps the file font-independent per hard-constraint and avoids the 'someone typed this' feel. Single-splash convention preserved: every ochre element is the same hue, only opacity / weight varies. Brass-bronze theme dot at (1148, 92) per family convention.
